speaker: interviewer
text: It's 5th of October, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Vera Gissing. And this is tape number 2. You were saying-- you were describing the room that the German officer wanted to take over for his office.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German commandant came to our house the very next day. And he summoned the whole family before him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I really hated to see him sitting there by our beautifully polished table. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: He had very shiny boots on his feet. And his heels, which were not all too clean, were resting on a rug which my mother had made herself. And, and I thought to myself, who is he to be lording it over our property in such a manner, into a room-- in a room which was forbidden to us children to play in because it was such a special room? How dare he take it over.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then he looked at my father, and he said to him, I hear that you and the family speak German. And my father said, that is correct. And the commandant went on. From now on I want only German spoken in this house. And my father looked him straight in the eye. He was-- Father was a very tall man, over six, six feet tall. And he said to him, I am the head of his household. And for as long as I live, we shall speak Czech, and German only in your presence. At that, the commandant stood up and spat in my father's face. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I stood there, horrified, watching the saliva run down his cheeks. And I vowed that I would never, ever speak another word of German again. And do you know, I truly cannot. I just wiped the knowledge of that language out of my mind. And I thought when I grew up that perhaps I ought to relearn it, and I've got such an aversion because of this experience that I gave up. But of course, that experience made me realize that the occupation was not a game and made me fear what else it might mean. And moreover, it made Mother realize that we were in danger. And after that, she left no stone unturned looking for a way to send me and my sister somewhere to safety. I know our parents talked about possibly leaving the country. But it was difficult for them. They didn't have that many contacts. But moreover, Mother just felt she could not leave her blind mother behind. And so they thought at least we should go to safety. My father was an optimist anyway. You know, he, he thought whatever might happen, it would be a-- if a war did come, it would be a storm in a teacup. And, and nobody really [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] had an inkling what terrible fate Hitler had in store for the Jews, not, not then, not in Czechoslovakia. Then my mother heard that there was a possibility of Jewish children coming to Britain on children transports. And she went to Prague and found out all about it, actually through Aunt Marta and through Aunt, Aunt, Aunt, Aunt-- through Uncle Gustav, who put the names of their sons down on this-- for the transports. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did, did you know at that time she was looking for something for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I had no idea. All I knew was that she had our photographs, my sister's and mine, professionally taken. But we didn't note for what purpose this was. We thought it was just for the family album. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My father didn't know, either, not until-- when was it? It must have been [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] April, May, I think, May 1939. We were sitting at the table having dinner. And my mother's plate remained untouched. And, and then she suddenly pushed her plate away and looked at father and said, I heard today that both Eva and Vera can go to England. And there was a deathly silence. And my father suddenly looked [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] very pale and drawn, and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he buried his face in his hands. And then he sighed and said, all right. Let them go. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a very, very strange movement and-- moment. And after that, I mean, I was filled with excitement and apprehension. I didn't know anyone from our town who'd ever been to England. You know, we-- suddenly my sister and I assumed great importance. And it seemed like a big, big adventure. And yet, we were both going to different places, I to a family in Liverpool I knew nothing about and my parents knew nothing about, and my sister to a school in Dorset. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were going to be separated, the two of us. You know, many years later, when I discovered the man who saved our lives and organized the transports, many people held it against him that he divided siblings, sent them to different places. And he said, so wisely and so rightly, if I'd waited for a family who was willing to take two refugee children for unlimited time, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you probably wouldn't be here today, because there were very few who would take more than one child. Just the same was his reason for accepting any family, whether Jewish or Christian, even those who, who gave the condition of acceptance conversion to Christianity, because he said, I was after saving lives, not souls. And this he did, very capably. But I'll come to the man later. He's not really a part of this scene, because my parents didn't know anything about him. By then, he was back in England looking for homes and, and families to, to be able to save as many endangered children as possible. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That was May-- as I said, it was end of May 1939 when we learned we could come. And after that, of course, there was frantic activity to get us ready. I remember Mother buying materials and taking us to the dressmaker and having a completely new wardrobe made for both of us to send us to England. And everything was made to fit. Nothing allowed for growth, because the only way they could-- our parents could sustain themselves, could sustain their optimism, was by saying to themselves and to us, you will be back within a year. You'll see. Had we entertained the possibility that we might be separated for years, or forever as was in our case, they probably would not have had the strength to do it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] When the moment finally came for us to leave, which was at the end of June, I had said goodbye to my school and my friends. This bosom pal of mine, Marta, is more than worth her mention. She was my shadow for many years and a real good friend of mine. And you know, my greatest passion in those early childhood days were cats. I used to collect everybody's cats off the streets, thinking they must be lost or hungry. And they weren't. And I had one cat who kept producing kittens. And her name was Julius. When we got her, we thought she was a male. And three kittens were born just shortly before we left. And I was so afraid my father would have them drowned. And Marta promised that she wouldn't let him drown them. And moreover, she promised she would visit my parents every single day so that they wouldn't miss me too much. And you know, she did that, even when it was forbidden for non-Jews to mix with Jews. In fact, one day, the local policeman visited her parents and said, you know, Marta is placing the whole family in danger by not obeying the laws, the German laws. You shouldn't let her visit the Diamants as she does. And her father said, she made a promise to her best pal. And no way am I going to make her break this promise. And I so valued what she did when I learned about it after the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just before we left, our thoughtful parents [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] gave me the most wonderful gifts. My father gave me a diary. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It's a-- it was a leather-bound book full of empty pages. And he told me to write it in when I was lonely or homesick or when I was happy, when I had something of interest to say, so that when I came back home, we could all sit around the table and read the diary together. And my mother, she carried me on that last night at home to an open window. And I remember the night sky was full of stars. And she said, there may be times when we can't write to one another. But even while we can, let the stars of the night and the sun of the day be the messengers of our thoughts and our love. And in that way, we always remained close. They couldn't have guessed what a godsend those thoughtful gestures would be, not just to me but to my sister, too. Because when war started and all correspondence ceased, we both poured our hearts into our diaries. And I, as a little girl every night, prayed to God and talked to Mother and Father through the stars. And if a falling star suddenly appeared, I thought, that must be a special messenger from Mother, you know, and it made all the difference. I never felt abandoned by them. I always felt close to them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember very clearly saying goodbye to Grandfather and Grandmother. And as he praised me and said, you are your mother's child and your father's child, and I know we will always be proud of you, and I felt, I've got to live so that they would be proud of me. And Grandmother, she went over my face with her hands so carefully as if memorizing every detail. It was very hard saying goodbye. My mother's sister, Auntie Berta, who was the youngest of the family, lived with them. She was a spinster, and she was living with them. She was the skinniest and the most unhealthiest of all the family. And she was very elegant. And she only tolerated me. She was very close to my sister because, as I said, she was the serious one and the older one and the dignified one. But as she sat between my sister and me in the back as the car sped to the station, I looked at her face, and I could see tears in her eyes. And I could see how pale she was. And I suddenly realized how much she loved us both. And, and I was glad that Mother had such a nice sister. And I was to be gladder still in latter years. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Saying goodbye at the Wilson Station in Prague in the middle of the night was very hard. The platform was filled with anxious parents and German soldiers. And Mother and Father bravely smiled at us until the time came for the train to leave. And I cried impulsively, see you again in a free Czechoslovakia. And suddenly everybody looked horrified and frightened. Luckily, the German soldiers didn't understand because they had instructions to, for any reasons, whatever to, to snatch a child and, you know, keep them there if anybody give any reasons. And as the train [INAUDIBLE] from the station, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my parents were crying, though they still tried to smile, you know, as they waved us goodbye. And suddenly I was so glad when my sister's hand reached for mine, and I realized I still had her. And she confirmed this in the same very words. She said, you'll always have me, come what may. You can always turn to me. I'll be there for you. And this sister whom I didn't particularly like, you know, being the elder bossy sister, suddenly changed into a guardian angel. And she remained so all through the years of the war. When we went through Germany and Holland, and-- the Dutch people gave us cocoa, I remember and some chocolate bars at the station. And of course, our parents had packed us-- packed rucksacks, rough food for both of us. So there was plenty of food on the journey. And then we got on a boat. And it was nighttime. And I could just hear the sea. I couldn't even see it. But early in the morning, I woke up and I could see-- through the little cubby hole, I could see the sun rising. And my sis woke up as well. And she sat there with me and put her arms around me. And we saw this glorious sun shining over the sea, our first sight of the sea and our first conversation with our mother and father through the sun. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wasn't able to go to my foster parents right from the word go. They didn't come for me. I was petrified because we all sat in this great big hole. And there were about 200 children on that transport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that hole?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was somewhere near-- what station? It was somewhere in London. I think it was somewhere near Bloomsbury-- no, it was near the station. Maybe it was on, on, on the station itself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which, which station?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Victoria Station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And name after name was called. And my sister had been called out. And she came back and just gave me into my hand the address of where she was going and said, write to me as soon as you arrive at your destination so I know where you are, too. And I just went on sitting there till I remained alone in this big hole. And I had this big tag around my neck. And then the leader of the transport who was in charge of the transport came to me and said, I had news that your foster parents can't come for you today. So you're coming with me. And I was taken in a big car to a huge house somewhere near a park. I presume it was Hyde Park. I, I really don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the man's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never learned the man's name. But he was either a bishop or archbishop, even. I tried later to find out who it could have been, but there were quite a few high dignitaries, dignitaries of the church involved in the rescue of children from occupied Europe. And I mean, it could have been-- you know, there was Archbishop of Canterbury, Archbishop of-- I, I don't know, there were, there were just so many. But this one had twin daughters. And they were, to me, grown-up daughters. They must have been about 18. And I was told that they had a, they had a Czech companion, which really thrilled me because I didn't know, didn't know a word of English except three phrases which my parents taught me. And anyway, when I got to this big house, which was on four floors, and they had a lift, and they had a, a, a butler and maids, you know, and a cook and everything, and I was taken to this very austere room with tapestries and very sort of sedate, old-fashioned colors, all reds and rusts. And it seemed very, very foreign to me. This lady appeared and said, you know-- greeted me in Czech. And I was so pleased. And she was the companion of the, of those daughters of this bishop or whoever he was. And she spoke monstrously bad Czech.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, how, how long have you been here? And she said, oh, I've been here a year. And I said, you've forgotten so much Czech. And she said, you wait. When you have been here a year, at your age, you will have forgotten every word.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was horrified of that. And just as I was determined to forget my German, I was equally determined never to get a single word of Czech. Of course, she did me a good turn because all through the years, I read the few books I brought with me from A to Z and Z to A. And of course I wrote to my parents. And after war started, just went on writing into my diaries and talking to my doll and talking to the stars and to the moon. And, and I, I never forgot a word. But this lady actually came from Sudetenland and probably went to German schools and probably never could speak Czech properly. But she didn't like to tell me that. I stayed two nights with this, with this family. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The other thing which really upset me was that when I had my first meal-- and I was a ravenous. And I had my meals, meal with these twins. And I expected children. And they were grownup ladies. And I was given wide bread. And the bread seemed like sponge. It was so soft and doughy and horrible. I was used to lovely firm rye Czech bread. And then I thought, well, at least I'll put lashings of butter on it. But the Czech butter didn't have any salt, and the English butter was all salty. And I had such a sweet tooth. Never mind, I thought. They looked a nice jam there. And when they said marmalade, which in Czech means jam, I thought, great. And I put lashings of what I thought was sweet jam. And it turned out to be orange marmalade. And I couldn't-- I just could not swallow a single mouthful. And I was so hungry. And I didn't know what to do.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I put a napkin on my lap. And every time they weren't looking, I popped a bit of the bread onto the napkin and then later on flushed the whole lot down the loo and looked forward to eating the Czech bread, which was left in my rucksack. But unfortunately, the leader of the transport, who was also staying there, had gave, gave instructions for Czech food to be destroyed, that now we are in England, we're going to eat English food only.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was devastated, and I was very cross with him. And I thought he was very badly behaved young man because we Czechs always said that bread is sacred and you must never waste any. And in, in fact, if we dropped a piece of bread, we had to kiss it. And so I was most, most indignant. And I quite forgot what I did with the English bread, how that ended. Anyway, my little English mummy, who next month is celebrating her 100th birthday, came to fetch, fetch me in Bloomsbury House two days later. I can remember that day as if it was yesterday because I was taken into an empty room and left standing there. And I mean, can you imagine, just before my 11th birthday, waiting there to-- for someone you're going to be living with who will be in charge of your life, whom you've never met. And there was just a little chair there and my rucksack. And I stood there and waited for the door to open. And in she came, this little lady, hardly taller than myself. And she had a hat on her head. And it was set all askew on it. And she had a long Mackintosh, and it was buttoned up all wrong and big glasses. And she peered at me from behind these big glasses. But her face broke into the biggest smile you ever saw and she started [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] shouting and crying all at the same time as she ran towards me and hugged me and spoke to me words I didn't understand then. But I learned since that they were, you shall be loved. And loved I was. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The family was relatively poor, but they had a, a heart of gold.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she have any children of their own?

speaker: interviewee
text: She had a child, Dorothy, who was three years older than I. So I gained not just an English mum and dad but an English sister. Her name was Dorothy. And she was waiting for me at the bus stop. She had waited for three hours impatiently while we made our way by train to Liverpool and then to Bootle where they lived. Very pretty gal with very long pigtails I very much admired and envied. I was always envying older children some attributes which I did not have. Her, her grandparents were there to greet me as well. But we came to this little semi-detached with these little poky rooms, you know, and old-fashioned furniture. It seemed so strange to me. But they wormed their way into my heart immediately. It happened to be my birthday when I arrived. And there were lashings of wonderful creamy ice cream and strawberries, which made up a lot for that horrible bread and marmalade and salty butter [LAUGHS] I had the day before. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was very happy with the family. But I didn't stay long because by the end of July, the threat of war was sort of imminent. And there was talk of evacuation of the children out of Liverpool. I did spend, of course, a month in the school, where they looked at me as if I was a creature from out of space because nobody had heard or seen anyone from Czechoslovakia, you know. And there was-- I, I remember the streets were lined with kids curious to see me. And I remember hearing the sound of my-- the echoes of my feet and Mummy Rainford's feet-- I called her Mummy Rainford and Daddy Rainford, you know, from the word go-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] as they led me, as she led me, you know, to the, to the school. So just a few weeks after arriving, I had to move again to another family in Southport.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before we get on to that, during that month, did you learn any English?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I picked up English very quickly. I was determined, also, to teach the family Czech, at which I was not so successful. But my English sister, particularly, was a great teacher and took great pains in explaining things to me. And I went-- when I went to the school, they put me with seven-year-olds because I didn't understand any English. And I didn't like that at all. But their teacher spoke German. And though I wouldn't speak, of course I did understand. And so she explained things to me, too. But I only stayed in that class for two weeks, and then I moved to, to a class of my own age group. I knew I was going to live in the place. And I knew I just had to learn as quickly as possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry to interrupt you, but we're at the end of the second tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I thought.
